name: Beetroot
summary: A root vegetable exceptionally high in dietary nitrate — the compound behind its best-studied effect: lowering blood pressure.
superfoodReason: One of the richest dietary sources of inorganic nitrate, with repeated randomized trials showing measurable blood-pressure reductions from a single glass of juice.

benefits.mechanism: Beetroot is rich in inorganic nitrate. Mouth bacteria reduce it to nitrite, which the body converts to nitric oxide — a signalling molecule that relaxes and widens blood vessels (vasodilation), lowering the pressure blood exerts on artery walls.

citations.title: Acute blood pressure lowering, vasoprotective, and antiplatelet properties of dietary nitrate via bioconversion to nitrite (Webb et al., Hypertension 2008)
Dietary nitrate provides sustained blood pressure lowering in hypertensive patients: a randomized, phase 2, double-blind, placebo-controlled study (Kapil et al., Hypertension 2015)

benefits.claim: May improve exercise stamina and oxygen efficiency
benefits.mechanism: The same nitrate-to-nitric-oxide pathway improves blood flow to working muscles and reduces the oxygen cost of exercise, so a given effort feels easier — an effect measured in endurance-performance trials.

citations.title: Dietary nitrate supplementation reduces the O2 cost of low-intensity exercise and enhances tolerance to high-intensity exercise in humans (Bailey et al., J Appl Physiol 2009)

surprisingFacts.text: The deep red colour comes from betalain pigments — the same family used as a natural food dye (E162), and studied in their own right as antioxidants.

cautions.effect: Pink or deep-red urine, and sometimes reddish stool, a day or so after eating beets. It is harmless and it is the pigment, not blood — though it startles people. It happens to roughly 10–14% of people, more often if iron stores are low.
